does: This paper identifies and formalizes authorization propagation as a distinct security problem in multi-agent AI systems, separate from prompt injection vulnerabilities. The authors define authorization propagation as the challenge of maintaining authorization invariants as non-human agents retrieve data, delegate tasks, and synthesize results across system boundaries. They decompose this into three core sub-problems: transitive delegation (controlling how authorization spreads through chains of agent actions), aggregation inference (preventing agents from inferring information beyond their authorization scope by combining multiple queries), and temporal validity (ensuring authorizations remain appropriate as execution contexts change). The paper derives seven structural requirements for authorization architectures that can address these challenges and surveys recent technical approaches including invocation-bound capability tokens, task-scoped authorization envelopes, dependency-graph policy enforcement, and execution-count revocation mechanisms.
connects: This work extends classical access-control frameworks—role-based access control (RBAC), attribute-based access control (ABAC), and relationship-based access control (ReBAC)—which were designed for human-centric or simple client-server systems and cannot fully capture the authorization problems unique to multi-agent workflows. It builds on foundational security concepts around delegation and revocation but adapts them to the novel context where non-human principals autonomously orchestrate multi-step operations across system boundaries. The paper responds to the broader security discourse around agentic AI, which has focused primarily on prompt injection as the dominant threat model, and argues for treating authorization management as a first-class infrastructure problem alongside that conversation. It also synthesizes insights from recent work on capability tokens, scoped authorization envelopes, and policy enforcement mechanisms that have emerged as the field tacitly recognizes authorization propagation challenges.
enables: This work establishes a framework that enables the development of comprehensive identity governance infrastructure for multi-agent AI systems. By formalizing authorization propagation and deriving architectural requirements, the paper provides a blueprint for building authorization systems that can safely support agentic automation at scale. It opens pathways for designing authorization mechanisms that decouple authorization decisions from agent behavior, allow fine-grained task-scoped permissions, and maintain audit trails of agent actions across delegation chains. The structural requirements serve as evaluation criteria for authorization technologies and guide engineers in building systems where agents can collaborate and delegate without creating authorization leakage. Future work can build complete reference implementations of these requirements, develop formal verification methods for authorization policies in multi-agent workflows, and establish standards that enable authorization interoperability across different agent platforms and organizations.
